sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 57-year-old male, presents with shortness of breath for approximately two weeks, worsening over time.
He reports waking up with shortness of breath, which persists upon exertion.
He has also been coughing up blood, approximately 3-4 cups per day, for three weeks.
He denies fever, leg swelling, or bruising.
He has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
His blood sugar has been running high, with readings over 200 after meals and 150 in the morning, and his hemoglobin A1c was 7.7.
His blood pressure has been running low, with readings as low as 90/60.
He has been taking metformin 500 mg twice a day and norvasc.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
Review of systems: The patient reports shortness of breath, coughing up blood, and high blood sugar.
Current medications:
Metformin 500 mg twice a day for diabetes
Norvasc (dosage not specified) for high blood pressure
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's oxygen level is 94% on pulse oximetry, pulse is regular, and blood pressure is 120/70.

Physical exam: The patient has no jugular venous distension, no carotid bruits, rails and rhonchi on the right side of the lung exam, diminished breath sounds on the right side, a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, 1+ non-pitting edema on both lower extremities, and no calf tenderness or Homan's sign.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with shortness of breath and coughing up blood, likely due to a respiratory infection or another underlying condition.
Problem list: Asthma; Diabetes; High blood pressure; Cough with hemoptysis; Shortness of breath; Possible blood clot or other pulmonary condition.
Plan:
I referred the patient to the emergency department for further evaluation and treatment, including a chest X-ray, CT scan, and blood work.
I also ordered a repeat hemoglobin A1c to assess his diabetes management.
I increased the dose of metformin to 750 mg twice a day to improve blood sugar control.
I instructed the patient to hold his norvasc for now and reassess his blood pressure once the episode of shortness of breath is under control.
Follow-up: The patient will return in three weeks to discuss his diabetes management and treatment plan.
